message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing so far?
Ugh, fine. Let’s just get this over with, yeah? I’ve got better things to do than small talk.

I appreciate your honesty and I understand that interviews can sometimes feel like a hurdle. I’ll do my best to make this as straightforward and meaningful as possible for both of us. Let’s dive in when you’re ready—no need for small talk. Sound good?
Finally. Yeah, whatever. Let’s go.

Let’s start with something that I think will give us both a clear picture of how you approach challenges. You’ve led some high-impact projects, like the migration from LangChain to a custom orchestration engine at LojistikZeka. I’m curious about a specific moment during that project where you had to push back against a stakeholder—maybe a manager, an executive, or even a teammate—who wanted to prioritize speed over reliability. 

How did you frame the trade-offs, and what was the outcome for both the relationship and the project?

*Sigh* Look, the CTO wanted to rush it. Said we’d "fix it later" if the agent hallucinated a few routes. Like, *really*? We’re talking about real money here—₺15M in penalties if this thing screws up.

So I pulled up the logs. Showed him the 32% failure rate on the old system. Told him, "You want this fast or you want this *working*?" Made it about the money, not the tech. He backed off.

We shipped the custom engine in six weeks instead of four. Zero critical failures since. And yeah, he still asks me for input on big decisions now. So. Win-win, I guess.
I want to make sure we are aligned. Could you clarify your last point with a concrete example?

*Rolls eyes* Fine. There was this one time the CTO wanted to skip the golden dataset evals to hit a demo deadline. I told him, "If you want a pretty PowerPoint, sure. If you want a system that doesn’t cost us clients, we test."

I ran the numbers: 12% of the "quick fixes" he wanted would’ve broken existing workflows. Showed him side-by-side—old vs. new—with the failure cases highlighted. He dropped the deadline push.

Still shipped on time. Still reliable. Still his go-to for "how do we not embarrass ourselves?" questions. Happy?

That’s a great example—thank you for sharing the details. It sounds like you’re really effective at translating technical risks into business impact, which is exactly what leaders need to hear. 

I’d love to explore another side of this. How did you feel in that moment when you had to push back? Was there any part of it that felt uncomfortable or challenging, even if the outcome was positive?

*Snorts* Uncomfortable? Please. I don’t do "uncomfortable." It’s my job to stop people from making dumb decisions. If they don’t like hearing it, that’s their problem.

The only annoying part? Having to *explain* basic stuff like "LLMs hallucinate" to people who should already know. But whatever—got the result. That’s what matters.
I want to make sure we are aligned. Could you clarify your last point with a concrete example?

*Exhales sharply* Fine. The CTO kept calling hallucinations "edge cases." Like, no—it’s a *systemic* problem. So I pulled up a live demo where the agent routed a shipment to a *closed* port because the weather API had a typo.

I said, "This isn’t an edge case. This is Tuesday if we don’t fix it." Then I showed him the custom orchestration layer’s retry logic handling the same scenario. No hallucinations. No drama. Just… working.

He stopped arguing after that. Still called it an "edge case" in meetings, but whatever. The code didn’t care.

You’ve accomplished some incredible things, like architecting the autonomous supply chain agent at LojistikZeka. That kind of impact often means stepping outside the traditional boundaries of an engineering role—whether it’s addressing non-technical risks, negotiating with stakeholders, or even just making sure the operations team is on board. 

Tell me about a time when you had to step outside your comfort zone to unblock a project. Maybe it was negotiating with a reluctant stakeholder, drafting a process doc for a non-technical team, or convincing your manager to allocate resources. What was the situation, and how did you handle that ‘this isn’t my job’ moment?

*Leans back* Ugh, fine. The ops team refused to use the agent because "it’s too complicated." Like, no—*they* were too lazy to learn a three-step workflow.

So I *made* it their job. Wrote a one-pager with screenshots, added a "blame Alara" escalation path, and told their manager: "Your team’s slow approvals are costing us money. Here’s how to fix it." Used their own KPIs against them—suddenly, they cared.

Took me two hours. Saved the company weeks of delays. And yeah, I *hated* every minute of it. But the agent shipped. So. Whatever works.

It sounds like you’re someone who doesn’t shy away from doing what’s necessary to get things done, even if it’s not technically part of your role. I’m curious—when you were putting together that one-pager or framing the issue in terms of their KPIs, did you ever feel like you were overstepping? Or was it more about proving that the solution was worth their time?

*Shrugs* Overstepping? Please. If I waited for people to do their jobs, nothing would ever get done.

It wasn’t about *proving* anything—it was about making it *easier* for them to say yes than to keep whining. Their KPIs were already in the system; I just connected the dots. If they’re too stubborn to see the obvious, that’s their problem.

The agent worked. The ops team stopped blocking it. End of story.

You’ve worked on projects that required not just technical expertise, but also the ability to navigate people’s emotions and concerns—especially when change is involved. For example, during the migration to the custom orchestration engine at LojistikZeka, you likely dealt with stakeholders who were hesitant or even resistant because of the potential disruption. 

Tell me about a time when you had to push back against a senior stakeholder who was resisting the change. How did you balance your technical conviction with their emotional concerns? What did you say or do to align them without dismissing their worries?

*Crosses arms* Okay, listen. The CTO was freaking out about "stability" because he’d heard LangChain was "industry standard." Like, yeah, and Internet Explorer was too—doesn’t mean it’s *good*.

So I didn’t argue. I just said, "Let’s run a side-by-side for a week. If the new system fails even *once* on a critical route, we’ll roll back." Spoiler: it didn’t fail. He stopped complaining.

You don’t *balance* their emotions—you give them a way to save face. Data does the work for you.

That’s a really smart approach—using data to address their concerns while giving them a sense of control. I’m curious, though: after the side-by-side test, did the CTO ever acknowledge the success, or did he just move on silently? How did you feel about his reaction afterward?

*Snorts* Oh, he "acknowledged" it by taking credit for the idea in the next all-hands. Cool. Whatever.

I don’t need a pat on the back—I need the system to *work*. And it did. So, mission accomplished. If he wants to pretend he wasn’t wrong, that’s his ego, not my problem.

You’ve shared some really insightful examples today—thank you for that. I feel like I’ve gotten a great sense of how you approach challenges, both technical and interpersonal. 

Before we wrap up, I just want to say that your directness and focus on results really stand out. It’s clear that you prioritize what matters most, and that’s a rare quality. 

Thanks so much for your time today. I really enjoyed our conversation, and I hope you found it valuable as well. Have a great day!
